text:
Unless you lived through itГўВҖВ”ideally while wearing parachute pantsГўВҖВ”you might have a hard time fully grasping just how omnipresent breaking was in 1980s pop culture. There were movies based on it, likeBeat Street,BreakinГўВҖВҷand its successor,BreakinГўВҖВҷ 2: Electric Boogaloo, whose subtitle has lived on for decades as a punch line for pretty much every sequel made. There were obligatory breaking scenes in moviesГўВҖВ”Cocoon,Revenge of the Nerdsand countless othersГўВҖВ”that otherwise had nothing to do with breaking. The sitcomAlicehad a breaking episode. Merv Griffin had a crew on his show. Barney Rubble showed off his moves in a commercial for Cocoa Pebbles. It waseverywhere.

Naturally, politicians saw a popular trend and were eager to get in on the action. So it was that at his second inauguration gala, in January 1985, a tuxedoed Ronald Reagan watched as Jimmy Stewart introduced the New York City Breakers, prompting a nation of viewers to ask themselves,How the hell did we get here?The answer only makes the story stranger (and whiter and older): It was Frank SinatraГўВҖВҷs idea. ГўВҖВңFrank was a hoofer,ГўВҖВқ says Michael Holman, who founded and managed the group. ГўВҖВңHe came from a tradition where you had to sing and dance. You couldnГўВҖВҷt just be a singer. He loved us.ГўВҖВқ

OlГўВҖВҷ Blue Eyes had first encountered the Breakers in late 1983 at the Kennedy Center Honors, where they performed as part of a tribute to the dancer Katherine Dunham. When it came time to book acts for ReaganГўВҖВҷs party, Sinatra, who was organizing the event, reached out to Holman. A San Francisco native, Holman had moved to New York City in ГўВҖВҷ78 to take a job as a banker. He hated the job but loved the city, where he was becoming a fixture in the downtown art scene while also immersing himself in the nascent world of hip-hop. He started an experimental noise band with artist Jean-Michel Basquiat. He took Malcolm McLarenГўВҖВ”the British impresario who created the Sex PistolsГўВҖВ”up to the Bronx to meet Afrika Bambaataa. While running a weekly hip-hop night at an East Village reggae club called Negril, Holman saw the crowd go wild over a breaking battle between two crews, the Floor Masters and the Rock Steady Crew, which gave him the inspiration to form the New York City Breakers.ГӮ

In less than a year they were booking gigs all over the place. Among the many TV spots that featured HolmanГўВҖВҷs crew was one that aired in 1984. In it, one of the Breakers, a B-Boy named Chino Lopez (better known as Action), threw down a gauntlet: ГўВҖВңMe and my whole crew, we challenge the 1984 [Olympic] gold medal winners in the floor exercise gymnastics team in a competition. WeГўВҖВҷll see you in L.A.ГўВҖВқ

Lamentably, the world never got to see that showdown, and by the middle of ГўВҖВҷ85 the mainstream breaking scene had dried up almost as quickly as it had sprung up. But 40 years later, ActionГўВҖВҷs wish has come true. Breaking (itГўВҖВҷs breaking, not breakdancing, by the way) is making its Games debut in Paris, but not without questions from those within the community. Like, does something that started as an art form, born on the streets of the Bronx in the 1970s in a neighborhood that was largely disenfranchised, belong at something as corporate as theOlympics?

Sunny Choi had Olympic dreams as a kid growing up in Louisville, but she saw herself as a gymnast, not a dancer. By the time she enrolled at Penn she was burned out on the sport. Without it, though, she was lacking direction. One night on the way home from a party she came across a group of breakers on campus. They had a club and convinced her to come to a free class a few days later, and she quickly got hooked.

ГўВҖВңThe reason I fell in love with it was because it was so different from gymnastics,ГўВҖВқ Choi says. ГўВҖВңMy whole life I had been living by this very, very organized list of checked boxes. What I loved about breaking is that it challenged that. It requires you to show up and to figure out who you are and express yourself.ГўВҖВқ And no matter how she expressed herself, she was accepted. In that world, she says, ГўВҖВңYou blend in by not fitting in.ГўВҖВқГӮ

Choi, 35, graduated from college in 2011. She got into marketing, eventually making her way up to global creative operations director for EstГғВ©e Lauder. She kept breaking, working out whenever she could find the time in her busy schedule. For 10 years she deftly moved between two worlds, one corporate, one hip-hop. After the IOC announced in 2020 that breaking would be added to the program in Paris, Choi used it as motivation to go all in. She quit her job, becoming B-Girl Sunny full-time.

Soon Choi was ranked in the worldГўВҖВҷs top 10. She clinched her spot in the Olympics when she won gold at the 2023 Pan Am Games. (A country can send up to two B-Girls and two B-Boys; the other U.S. competitor who directly qualified is Victor Montalvo.)

Like Choi, CanadaГўВҖВҷs B-Boy Philip Kim, who also earned a ГўВҖВҷ23 Pan Am Games gold medal, stumbled into the sport by chancing upon some breakers. In KimГўВҖВҷs case, it was outside an art museum in his hometown of Vancouver. Replicating the crewГўВҖВҷs moves gave the middle schooler an outlet for his ample energy.ГӮ

ГўВҖВңI was super into anime and so were a lot of my friends,ГўВҖВқ he says. ГўВҖВңSo we would make up characters and play at recess. And I did the same at home. My parents remember me running around in my underwear at home, pretending to be Goku or Spider-Man.ГўВҖВқ

By the time he was in ninth grade, his classmates had dubbed him B-Boy Kimchi. (HeГўВҖВҷs since adopted a new moniker: Phil Wizard, the surname coming from a crew he used to be a part of.) Becoming a professional was a no-brainer, and not just because heГўВҖВҷs good (he was the 2022 world champion). His career allows him to connect with those superhero days. ГўВҖВңFor me, now, breaking is reminiscent of that in a way, because I just kind of travel the world, doing dance battles,ГўВҖВқ he says.

Breaking has always had a competitive element. ГўВҖВңIf you were poor, you got your best clothes on and your best cologne, youГўВҖВҷre styling,ГўВҖВқ says Michael Chambers, a B-Boy known as Boogaloo Shrimp and one of the stars of 1984ГўВҖВҷsBreakinГўВҖВҷand its sequel. ГўВҖВңIf youГўВҖВҷre styling and you ainГўВҖВҷt got much money and you took a bus to the club, you could still be the king of the city if you had the right talent. The battling thing was like,O.K., IГўВҖВҷm going to bruise your ego. I may be broke, but IГўВҖВҷm no joke. Watch this. Boom!ГўВҖВқ (It bears noting that Chambers, who last year put out a kids book calledLilГўВҖВҷ Boogaloo Shrimp and the Clean Sweep,would like to be the Charles Barkley of breaking.)

When Chambers and fellow breaker Adolfo QuiГғВұones threw down against the Electro Rock on film (with a young Ice-T as the DJ), the people in the room decided who won, crowning the winner by acclamation. The sport of Olympic breaking is a wholly different animal.

The competitors face off head-to-head, alternating turns in a series of three throw downs, each of which can last up to a minute. A panel of judges declares the winner. There are no required moves and while it is literally dancing, itГўВҖВҷs rarely choreographed. ГўВҖВңI only plan about 20 to 30% of a round,ГўВҖВқ says Choi. ГўВҖВңBut even then, sometimes that goes out the window and IГўВҖВҷll change it right on the spot.ГўВҖВқ

Montalvo, 30, who competes as B-Boy Victor, will plan out three signature moves in each throw down. ГўВҖВңI try to keep myself 50% structured and then 50% improvised,ГўВҖВқ he says.

Part of that improvisation is going with the flow, but thereГўВҖВҷs also a strategic element at play. ГўВҖВңOften times IГўВҖВҷm responding to the opponent,ГўВҖВқ says Kim. ГўВҖВңIn breaking we often talk about having a conversation with movement. If someone does a move but I have a better variation of it, itГўВҖВҷs also showing the judges,Look, heГўВҖВҷs adapting in the moment. HeГўВҖВҷs also showing that heГўВҖВҷs listening to the opponent.ГўВҖВқГӮ

Kim has an especially deep repertoire of movesГўВҖВ”avocabulary, in the sportГўВҖВҷs parlanceГўВҖВ”which makes him tough to beat, since judges are on the lookout for a variety of techniques, including:

ГўВҖВңYouГўВҖВҷre using every muscle in your body, basically, in a way that you donГўВҖВҷt in your everyday life,ГўВҖВқ says Choi. ГўВҖВңYouГўВҖВҷre not just walking on your feet. YouГўВҖВҷre upside down, youГўВҖВҷre rotating, youГўВҖВҷre spinning, youГўВҖВҷre sliding.ГўВҖВқ

That means that in addition to dance work, thereГўВҖВҷs a lot of time spent in the gym. ГўВҖВңItГўВҖВҷs usually a combination of strength training, explosive work, high intensity interval trainingГўВҖВ”things that will help my breaking as well as exercises that will work the antagonist muscles,ГўВҖВқ says Kim. ГўВҖВңBreaking is a lot of push movements. So I will incorporate a lot of pull movements into my workouts so that IГўВҖВҷm not imbalanced with my body.ГўВҖВқ

While the goal of a breaker is to exert total control over their body, thereГўВҖВҷs one thing they have zero control of: the music. Unlike gymnasts or figure skaters, who arrive with predetermined music, B-Boys and B-Girls are at the mercy of the DJ, who picks the tunes. The only thing competitors know ahead of time is a rough range of beats per minute.

Not only does the music selection make preparation tricky, itГўВҖВҷs also a source of controversy. In Paris, because of copyright laws, you wonГўВҖВҷt hear any old-school hip-hop, which feeds into the main argument by critics of breakingГўВҖВҷs inclusion in the Olympics: that not enough is done to connect the sport to its roots, especially when it will draw millions of eyeballs.

Gabriel Dionisio, better known as Kwikstep, grew up breaking in New York City in the 1980s. He finds himself frustrated by way the sport is being presented. ГўВҖВңIГўВҖВҷm excited by [the Olympics] and its potential of exposing breaking further to the world,ГўВҖВқ he says. ГўВҖВңWhat IГўВҖВҷm concerned with is the lack of cultural representation. Its origins, where it comes from.ГўВҖВқ

With his wife, Ana Garcia (the groundbreaking B-Girl Rokafella), Dionisio runs a non-profit, Full Circle Productions, that procures grant money from the likes of the Ford Foundation and the Mellon Foundation. They use the funds to help put on shows and to educate young people about the roots of breaking. Ahead of an event Dionisio will make sure the MC knows the stories of any old school B-Boys who are there, and he sets up a booth where crews can put up old photos. ГўВҖВңNow it has a social context,ГўВҖВқ he says.

Ken Gabbert is a little more blunt. As Ken Swift, he was a pioneering member of the Rock Steady Crew. ГўВҖВңItГўВҖВҷs not a sport,ГўВҖВқ he says. ГўВҖВңAn art form that we created with our own terminology and heritage and tradition, all of a sudden for an opportunity, we start calling ourselves athletes and calling this art a sport? ThatГўВҖВҷs like saying, ГўВҖВҳLetГўВҖВҷs call basketball a dance now after 75 years.ГўВҖВҷ As a part of a culture, I look at it from a different perspective. I look at it artistically, as something that weГўВҖВҷve sustained for 50 years to get to this point.

ГўВҖВңI love sports. I get that. But when I was breaking, the whole aspect of doing stuff with my bodyГўВҖВ”the type of freedom, really connecting to drums and musicГўВҖВ”was different for me. So right now I see whatГўВҖВҷs happening. A lot of people act like IГўВҖВҷm a hater, but theyГўВҖВҷre wrong. ItГўВҖВҷs just that I have a culture IГўВҖВҷm a part of called hip-hop, and it ainГўВҖВҷt going to change.ГўВҖВқ

HolmanГўВҖВ”who speaks passionately about the community-building and storytelling power of breakingГўВҖВ”sees both sides of the argument. Ultimately heГўВҖВҷs in favor of the Olympics (he petitioned to have breaking included in 1984), but he does have concerns about where the sport is headed. ГўВҖВңA lot of these new jack, young breakers, many of them are these incredible gymnasts,ГўВҖВқ he says. ГўВҖВңItГўВҖВҷs not just gymnastics, itГўВҖВҷs also gyroscopic control over your own body. And theyГўВҖВҷre doing these moves that are just out of this world. But are they dancing to the music?

ГўВҖВңYou see them going into their routine and theyГўВҖВҷre on beat, but then theyГўВҖВҷre going into these moves that are living in their own time and space and have nothing to do with the beats and the rhythm and the melody and the energy and the emotional power of the song and of the music. TheyГўВҖВҷre just doing these moves and challenging someone else to do a move as powerful. You donГўВҖВҷt even need music to do it. If your routine can just be done without music, then thatГўВҖВҷs not breaking.ГўВҖВқ

Most young breakers are attuned to the discussion. Montalvo, whose father and uncle were B-Boys in Mexico, says he doesnГўВҖВҷt really like dancing to the music at competitions: ГўВҖВңГўВҖВүГўВҖВҳApacheГўВҖВҷ [by the Incredible Bongo Band] or James BrownГўВҖВҷs ГўВҖВҳBreak It LooseГўВҖВҷ remix? You put those on and thatГўВҖВҷll have us dancing all night.ГўВҖВқ

Choi, who aspires to open a dance and community center in Queens, says one thing she doesnГўВҖВҷt like in her role as B-Girl Sunny is doing press. But, she says, ГўВҖВңI do it a lot to share about breaking and the community, about the culture. You donГўВҖВҷt have breaking without hip-hop. You donГўВҖВҷt have breaking without the culture.ГўВҖВқ

ItГўВҖВҷs a point echoed by Kim, but being a 27-year-old Canadian means he doesnГўВҖВҷt have the personal experience of the old school, which in turn means heГўВҖВҷll more readily accept an evolution. ГўВҖВңIt comes from Black and Latino culture,ГўВҖВқ he says. ГўВҖВңThatГўВҖВҷs the roots of where it came from. But I think it also speaks on the beauty of where breaking and hip-hop in general can go.ГўВҖВқ

One place breaking, ironically, wonГўВҖВҷt go is back to the Olympics. ItГўВҖВҷs not on the docket for the 2028 Games, a head-scratching decision by the IOC given that theyГўВҖВҷre to be held in Los Angeles, one of the hotbeds of the early days of the art form. Keeping it on the program would give the sport a chance to grow its popularity further and maybe a second appearance, one without the novelty that comes with a debut, would mean more time could be spent on discussing the history. And breaking has always had a way of delivering memorable sequels.	ГӮ

Mark Bechtel has been a writer and editor at Sports Illustrated for 30 years. HeГўВҖВҷs covered a variety of sports, including baseball, auto racing, soccer andГўВҖВ”his favoriteГўВҖВ”curling, as well as contributing to the magazineГўВҖВҷs media coverage.

title: Breaking Will Make Its Olympic Debut in Paris, but Not Without Controversy
description: While some celebrate its inclusion in this summerГўВҖВҷs Games, others in the community say a corporate competItion isnГўВҖВҷt staying true to the art formГўВҖВҷs hip-hop roots.
